Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: Created by Author with ChatGPTIn Palantir’s nine-point AI sovereignty manifesto, Alex Karp and company make an argument that is almost impossible to disagree with on its surface. Institutions, they argue, should not casually hand over their data, their model weights, their operational knowledge, or their future to outside AI vendors. Data is treasure. Model weights shape destiny. Institutional knowledge is not just information sitting in a database. It is history, judgment, competitive advantage, and the accumulated intelligence of people who spent years building something that worked.
All of that is true, which is precisely what makes the manifesto so revealing.
Because the moment you accept Palantir’s argument, you have to apply it backward. If corporate data is sacred because it contains the accumulated intelligence of an institution, then the accumulated intelligence of writers, artists, journalists, musicians, photographers, researchers, coders, bloggers, and publishers was sacred too. Their work was not digital compost. It was not ownerless material floating around the internet waiting to be harvested by the first company with enough GPUs and legal confidence. It was human output. It was creative capital. It was someone’s livelihood, reputation, voice, skill, memory, and discipline.
The Sudden Discovery of Ownership
